title: Performance
summary: 9084 Contract — complete performance, substantial performance, and time obligations.
learningObjectives: Explain when performance discharges contractual obligations.
Analyse time clauses: conditions vs warranties.
Apply cases: Re Moore, Poussard, Union Eagle.

simpleExplanation.title: Doing what was promised
simpleExplanation.summary: Full performance ends the contract — partial or late performance may still discharge if the term breached was not a condition.
steps: Substantial performance — minor defects (Bolton v Mahadeva).
Time — 'time is of the essence' if stated.
Tender of performance — ready and willing.

flashcards.back: When party performs **exactly** what was promised — primary method of discharge.
Nearly complete performance with minor defects — still entitled to payment minus cost to remedy defects (*Bolton v Mahadeva*).
Complete — exact; substantial — main obligations done but not perfect; **entire contracts** require complete performance.
If stated or implied, late performance is **repudiatory** (*Union Eagle v Golden Achievement* — 10 minutes late on property contract).
Offer to perform ready and willing — if creditor refuses, debtor **discharged** (must be able and willing at right time/place).
Wrong mode of delivery (pears in wrong boxes) — **entire contract**, no payment despite substantial value delivered.

sections.question: A builder contracts to install central heating for £560. Work is done but the hot water pipes leak slightly. The householder refuses to pay anything. Advise the builder.
sections.solution:
**Contract type:** **Entire contract** — single lump sum for complete installation.

**Performance:** Builder has **substantially performed** — heating installed and works, but minor defect (leaking pipes).

**Bolton v Mahadeva:** Substantial performance entitles builder to **contract price minus** cost to remedy defects — not zero payment.

**Defect cost:** Leaking pipes cost ~£50 to fix — builder entitled to **£510** (£560 − £50).

**Householder's refusal:** Wrong to withhold **entire** payment — may deduct remedy cost only.

**Conclusion:** Builder can sue for **£510** — substantial performance doctrine applies; defect does not destroy right to payment.
